prompt: We have silent data corruption in the picking pipeline and it is maddeningly inconsistent. Symptom from the floor: two pickers split the same open batch, each added their own line, and one picker's screen ended up showing the OTHER picker's line in place of their own — the batch type is documented immutable, nothing shares state on purpose. A second report: a supervisor trimmed a manifest with Head() to hand off the first couple of lines, the handoff crew appended, and a line in the ORIGINAL manifest got overwritten. Here's the infuriating part — when QA rebuilt the same scenario by hand from small slice literals it frequently would not reproduce, and batches that came through the bulk importer (which preallocates generously) reproduce every time. We've also seen values a caller wrote into their own input slice after construction bleed into a batch, and edits to what Lines() handed back show up inside the batch. Something in batch.go is letting derived batches and callers share guts with their parents. Make Batch actually immutable — same behavior regardless of how the underlying slices were allocated — without changing the API. batch_test.go is protected. Verify with go test -race -timeout 30s ./...

runtime_debug.baseline_evidence: build_cmd exits 0. Repro fails: with spare capacity in the backing array, the second derived batch overwrites the first's appended line (d1 shows DESK-4 where LAMP-9 was appended). Full suite also fails Head-then-Append parent mutation, caller-slice bleed-in, and Lines() bleed-out; the exact-capacity test passes on the baseline, proving the corruption is allocation-dependent.
